introduction: Anaemia is the most common complication of pregnancy in sub-Saharan Africa. It is a diminution below normal of the total circulating haemoglobin mass. World Health Organization definition of anaemia. Haemoglobin concentration less than 11 g/dL or a haematocrit less than 33% in peripheral blood For practical purposes in developing and tropical countries, a haemoglobin concentration of 10 g/dL or haematocrit of 30% is taken as the lowest cut off limit. Below these levels there may be adverse foetal and maternal outcomes

features: Generalised weakness, lassitude and easy fatiguability
Jaundice may or may not be present
Anaemic heart failure in extreme cases

investigations: Stool analysis: ova, cysts, parasites, occult blood
Bone marrow biopsy if bone marrow involvement is suspected

non_drug: Prompt and appropriate treatment of febrile illnesses in pregnancy
Improvement of the socioeconomic status of the people
Provision of accessible and affordable maternity care facilities
drug: Oral haematinics: Ferrous sulphate 200 mg daily and folic acid 5 mg daily, Vitamin C 100 mg three times daily
Parenteral iron: Indicated in mild to moderate anaemia, near term, malabsorption of oral iron, or when it causes serious gastroenteritis
Blood transfusion: Consider as from the 37 week for mild anaemia and from the 32 week for moderate anaemia, usually packed cells under furosemide cover
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Antihistamine (chlorpheniramine injection), epinephrine and hydrocortisone injection must be available: iron dextran could cause severe anaphylaxis
supportive_measures: For each 1 g/dL deficit, 250 mg of iron dextran injection is required
Administer by deep intramuscular injection into the gluteal muscle, or by slow intravenous injection, or by intravenous Infusion (after a negative test dose)
Intramuscular injection: 250 mg daily after a negative test dose of 25 mg

Intravenous: If the total calculated dose of iron dextran is less than 1,500 mg, it can be given over 8 hours in one litre of sodium chloride (0.9% Normal Saline). If greater than 1,500 mg, it should be given in divided doses daily, not exceeding 1,500 mg/day

complications: Maternal complications: Abortion, Cardiac failure, Reduced ability to tolerate blood loss at delivery, Reduced ability to tolerate anaesthesia, Diminished resistance to infection, Preterm labour, Precipitate labour may occur, Death

Foetal complications: Spontaneous abortion, Intrauterine growth restriction, Intrauterine foetal death (leading to still birth), Prematurity, Risk of developing anaemia within 2 - 3 months of birth if mother suffered iron deficiency anaemia
prevention: Prompt and appropriate treatment of febrile illnesses in pregnancy
Improvement of the socioeconomic status of the people
Provision of accessible and affordable maternity care facilities
